elements.sent-2411-001: Click-through Rate (CTR) prediction is crucial for online personalization platforms.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recent advancements have shown that modeling rich user behaviors can significantly improve the performance of CTR prediction.
elements.sent-2411-003: Current long-term user behavior modeling algorithms predominantly follow two cascading stages.
elements.sent-2411-004: The first stage retrieves subsequence related to the target item from the long-term behavior sequence, while the second stage models the relationship between the subsequence and the target item.
elements.sent-2411-005: Despite significant progress, these methods have two critical flaws.
elements.sent-2411-006: First, the retrieval query typically includes only target item information, limiting the ability to capture the user's diverse interests.
elements.sent-2411-007: Second, relational information, such as sequential and interactive information within the subsequence, is frequently overlooked.
elements.sent-2411-008: Therefore, it requires to be further mined to more accurately model user interests.
elements.sent-2411-009: To this end, we propose Multi-granularity Interest Retrieval and Refinement Network (MIRRN).
elements.sent-2411-010: Specifically, we first construct queries based on behaviors observed at different time scales to obtain subsequences, each capturing users’ interest at various granularities.
elements.sent-2411-011: We then introduce an noval multi-head Fourier transformer to efficiently learn sequential and interactive information within the subsequences, leading to more accurate modeling of user interests.
elements.sent-2411-012: Finally, we employ multi-head target attention to adaptively assess the impact of these multi-granularity interests on the target item.
elements.sent-2411-013: Extensive experiments have demonstrated that MIRRN significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
elements.sent-2411-014: Furthermore, an A/B test shows that MIRRN increases the average number of listening songs by 1.32\% and the average time of listening songs by 0.55\% on a popular music streaming app.
elements.sent-2411-015: The implementation code is publicly available at \textcolor{blue {\url{https://github.com/psycho-demon/MIRRN .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Click-Through Rate (CTR) prediction is a crucial task in modern online personalization platforms~\cite{wang2021decoupled, wang2019mcne, wang2021hypersorec, zhang2024unified, wangmf , such as recommendation systems~\cite{tong2024mdap, wu2024survey, yin2024entropy and online advertising~\cite{oa1, oa2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Its objective is to estimate the probability of a user clicking on a specific item within a given contextual situation~\cite{ctr01, deepfm .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Recent advancements in CTR prediction have shown that user behavior modeling which incorporates the user's historical behavior sequence to learn implicit interests results in significant performance gain~\cite{ ctr3, DIN, DIEN .}
elements.sent-2411-019: As online platforms evolve, the length of user behavior sequences has experienced a surge.For instance, on Taobao, one of China's largest e-commerce platforms, over 23\% of users have recorded more than 1,000 clicks in the past five months alone~\cite{lifelong .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Effectively modeling this massive and intricate user behavior data has emerged as a focal point for both academia and industry.
elements.sent-2411-021: Due to the strict online inference time constraints, previous methods~\cite{DIN, DIEN, SAS considering their high time complexity, can only utilize the most recent behaviors with the sequence length seldom exceeding 100.}
elements.sent-2411-022: This limitation leads to suboptimal results.
elements.sent-2411-023: To address this, several models have been proposed to leverage long behavior sequences for modeling user interests.
elements.sent-2411-024: Most of these models follow two cascading stages~\cite{SIM .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In the first stage, the General Search Unit (GSU) identifies a small number of behaviors most relevant to the target item from thousands of long-term behaviors using fast and coarse search methods.
elements.sent-2411-026: In the second stage, the Exact Search Unit (ESU) captures the relationships between behaviors in these subsequences and the target item to precisely learn user interests.
elements.sent-2411-027: The most critical difference among these models such as SIM~\cite{SIM , UBR4CTR~\cite{UBR4CTR , ETA~\cite{ETA , SDIM~\cite{SDIM , UBCS~\cite{UBCS and TWIN~\cite{TWIN , lies in the different search strategies adopted by GSU.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Despite significant improvements in performance and efficiency, most two-stage models~\cite{UBR4CTR, SIM, ETA focus on limited contextual information in the first stage and neglect to mine the internal relations of subsequences in the second stage.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In the first stage, we argue that the retrieval process, where the retrieval query contains only the target item information, accounts solely for target-aware interest.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, if the retrieval query includes relevant information about behaviors at different time scales, more diverse user interests can be mined.
elements.sent-2411-031: Ignoring these "multi-granularity interests", weakens the representation power of the learned user interest.
elements.sent-2411-032: For example, consider the user behavior sequence shown in Figure ~\ref{fig:intro .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Most models capture target-aware interests, favoring "shoes" with a high probability when focusing on the target item "black sneakers".
elements.sent-2411-034: However, we believe that coarser-grained user interests, such as "local-aware" and "global-aware" interests, also impact the final prediction results.
elements.sent-2411-035: Recent clicks on "blue yoga pants" and "blue T-shirt" indicate the user's local-aware interest in "sports".
elements.sent-2411-036: Additionally, broader user behavior sequence indicates the user's global interest in "black" items.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this example, these interests in shoes, sports, and black all positively influence the click rate on black sneakers.
elements.sent-2411-038: In the second stage, we believe that the relational information in the subsequence, such as sequential and interactive information, is as valuable as that in the original sequence~\cite{SAS .}
elements.sent-2411-039: It needs to be further mined to model more accurate user interests.
elements.sent-2411-040: In fact, we identify two key challenges in the long-term behavior modeling that require further investigation: (1) Effectively capturing diverse user interests under stringent online inference time constraints presents a non-trivial challenge.

The middle column depicts the user's multi-granularity interests. The right column displays the subsequences related to the user's multi-granularity interests.} \label{fig:intro} \Description{}
elements.sent-2411-041: To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose Multi-granularity Interest Retrieval and Refinement Network (MIRRN).
elements.sent-2411-042: Firstly, we propose multi-granularity interest retrieval module (MIRM) to capture diverse user interests.
elements.sent-2411-043: MIRM constructs queries based on the behaviors observed at different time scales, which contain the target, local, and global information of the behavior sequence, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-044: Using these queries, MIRM adopts a fast search method based on SimHash to handle behavior sequences of thousands of lengths, obtaining subsequences that each contains interest of different granularities.
elements.sent-2411-045: Secondly, we propose Behavior Sequence Refinement Module (BSRM) to extract relational information in subsequences.
elements.sent-2411-046: To incorporate sequential information and efficiently mine interactive information, BSRM employs Target-Aware Position Encoding and Multi-Head Fourier Transformer (MHFT), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-047: MHFT utilizes the fast Fourier transform (FFT) to replace the complex convolution operation in the time domain, thereby enabling interaction with a low time complexity of $O(nlogn)$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Additionally, MHFT introduces matrix multiplication and multi-head mechanism to enhance interaction and focus on information in different subspaces, while greatly reducing the number of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-049: Finally, we propose multi-granularity interest activation module (MIAM) using the multi-head target attention to adaptively learn the influence of different granular interests on the target item, obtaining the final user representation.
elements.sent-2411-050: In summary, our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-051: In our proposed MIRRN, MIRM constructs various queries and then obtains subsequences through a fast retrieval method.
elements.sent-2411-052: This approach enables MIRM to effectively capture multi-granularity interests.
elements.sent-2411-053: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to successfully model the user’s multi-granularity interests in long-term user behavior modeling problem.
elements.sent-2411-054: In our proposed MIRRN, BSRM adopts the target-ware position encoding and the noval multi-head Fourier transformer (MHFT) to mine sequential and interactive information.
elements.sent-2411-055: MHFT innovatively utilizes the fast Fourier transform, matrix multiplication and multi-head mechanism to efficiently and comprehensively mine interactive information.
elements.sent-2411-056: Extensive experiments have demonstrated that MIRRN achieves better performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
elements.sent-2411-057: We verify the validity of each module through comprehensive ablation studies.
elements.sent-2411-058: Furthermore, an online A/B testing conducted on a popular music streaming app.
elements.sent-2411-059: Early CTR models primarily concentrate on exploiting feature interactions.
elements.sent-2411-060: Pioneering studies are relatively shallow such as factorization machines(FM)~\cite{FM and its variants~\cite{FM1, FM2, FM3, FM4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: With the widespread success of deep learning, various studies such as Wide\&Deep~\cite{wide , PNN~\cite{PNN , DeepFM~\cite{deepfm and DCN~\cite{DCN, DCN2 , successfully combine deep and shallow models.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Moreover, several models~\cite{AFM, NFM, xdeepfm, CELS, celslake have explored more complex neural networks, such as xDeepFM~\cite{xdeepfm further introduces compressed interaction networks to explicitly model high-order feature interactions.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: With the increasing demand for personalization, researchers are focusing on user behavior modeling~\cite{DIN, han2023guesr, yin2023apgl4sr, shen2024exploring, DIEN, TIN , which derives the latent representation of users' interest from their behavior sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-064: YoutubeDNN~\cite{youtube performs mean pooling on the entire user sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-065: DIN~\cite{DIN proposes an attention mechanism to identify target-relevant interests.}
elements.sent-2411-066: DIEN~\cite{DIEN introduces a novel GRU~\cite{gru with an attention update gate (AUGRU) to capture the evolving process of user interests.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: DSIN~\cite{DSIN observes that user behaviors are highly homogeneous in each session, and heterogeneous cross sessions.}
elements.sent-2411-068: DMIN~\cite{DMIN and MIND~\cite{mind employ self-attention mechanisms and capsule networks, respectively, to represent user interests as multiple vectors.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: CAN~\cite{CAN captures the feature co-action by directing user behavior features into a compact multi-layer perceptron generated by target features.}
elements.sent-2411-070: As user behavior modeling~\cite{yin2024learning, han2024efficient, wang2024denoising, xie2024breaking, yin2024dataset, xie2024bridging, DIN has demonstrated remarkable performance in industrial applications, researchers start to focus on modeling increasingly longer behaviors.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Due to strict limitations on inference time and model parameter count, DIN-like models~\cite{DIN,DIEN,DSIN are less suitable for long sequence modeling.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Recently, various models~\cite{MIMN, SIM, UBCS, ETA, SDIM, TWIN have been proposed to leverage long behavior sequences for modeling user interests.}
elements.sent-2411-073: MIMN~\cite{MIMN stores user behaviors in the memory matrix of the User Interest Center (UIC).}
elements.sent-2411-074: However, MIMN does not pay much attention to the target item information.
elements.sent-2411-075: SIM~\cite{SIM introduces a two-stage cascading framework to overcome above issue.}
elements.sent-2411-076: In the first stage, the General Search Unit (GSU) uses a rapid and coarse search method to retrieve several behaviors related to the target item from thousands of long-term behaviors, forming subsequences.
elements.sent-2411-077: The most critical difference between later models lies in the different search strategies adopted by GSU.
elements.sent-2411-078: For example, SIM Hard~\cite{SIM selects behaviors in the same category as the target item.}
elements.sent-2411-079: SIM Soft~\cite{SIM employs the inner product of pre-trained item embeddings as the relevance measure.}
elements.sent-2411-080: UBR4CTR~\cite{UBR4CTR utilizes BM25 algorithm to search for related behaviors.}
elements.sent-2411-081: ETA~\cite{ETA proposes an end-to-end model that leverages locality-sensitive hashing~\cite{simmm (LSH) to encode item embeddings and retrieve relevant items via Hamming distance.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: SDIM~\cite{SDIM directly gathers behavior items that share the same hash signature with the candidate item, then performs a linear aggregation on them.}
elements.sent-2411-083: UBCS~\cite{UBCS adopts a sampling method that considers relevance and temporal information and accelerates the sampling process through clustering.}
elements.sent-2411-084: TWIN~\cite{TWIN designed the Consistency-Preserved GSU (CP-GSU) by behavior feature splitting, using the same target behavior correlation measure as ESU.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Additionally, several approaches attempt to directly shorten the length of user behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-086: ADFM~\cite{ADFM uses hierarchical aggregation to compress raw behavior sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-087: DGIN~\cite{DGIN groups behaviors using a relevant key to remove abundant items.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Based on TWIN, TWIN-V2~\cite{TWIN2 further employs a hierarchical clustering method to group items with similar characteristics into a cluster.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Nevertheless, these models overlook the diversity of user interests and the relationships among behaviors in the subsequences.
elements.sent-2411-090: Our MIRRN focuses on learning multi-granular user interests and efficiently mining sequential and interactive information .
elements.sent-2411-091: The framework of MIRRN is depicted in Figure~\ref{model .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Our model comprises Embedding Layer, Multi-granularity Interest Retrieval Module, Behavior Sequence Refinement Module, Multi-granularity Interest Activation Module and Prediction Layer.
elements.sent-2411-093: The details of these modules will be introduced in the subsequent subsections.
elements.sent-2411-094: Depending on the nature of the input features, we utilize diverse embedding techniques.
elements.sent-2411-095: We categorize input features into two main types: categorical and numeric features.
elements.sent-2411-096: We perform one-hot encoding and multi-hot encoding on categorical features.
elements.sent-2411-097: For numerical features, we first assign them to different numerical buckets and then perform one-hot encoding to distinguish these buckets.
elements.sent-2411-098: It's typical to map all one-hot feature vectors into a lower-dimensional latent vector space.
elements.sent-2411-099: Specifically, we define the embedding vector of the $i$-th behavior in the user behavior sequence $b_i$ as $e_i \in \mathbb{R ^{d}$, where $d$ represents the embedding size.}
elements.sent-2411-100: We consolidate all the embedding vectors of user behavior items into a matrix $S \in \mathbb{R ^{L \times d}$, representing the embedding representation of the user behavior sequence: \begin{equation}S = [e_1; e_2; \dots; e_L].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-101: We denote the embedding of the target item as $e_t$.
elements.sent-2411-102: For other features, we abbreviate them as $E_{other}$.
elements.sent-2411-103: Considering the strict limitations on inference time and the number of model parameters for long sequence modeling, we follow previous work~\cite{ETA using a simple and fast search method to filter out relevant items from the user's long-term behavior sequence to obtain subsequence (SBS).}
elements.sent-2411-104: We then accurately model the user's interests within SBS to reduce training and inference costs.
elements.sent-2411-105: However, in previous work, the query in the retrieval stage considers only the target information, resulting in SBS that contain only the user's target-relevant interest.
elements.sent-2411-106: We construct queries based on behaviors observed at different time scales, containing target, local, and global information from the behavior sequence, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-107: These queries serve as microcosms of the user's diverse interests, allowing the retrieved SBSs to contain interests of different granularities.
elements.sent-2411-108: Below, we will introduce the methods for constructing different queries and the fast search units in detail.
elements.sent-2411-109: The information contained in the target item is crucial for accurately predicting the user's click rate on it.
elements.sent-2411-110: Therefore, in this unit, we directly use the embedding of the target item as the query $q_{t}$.
elements.sent-2411-111: Using this query, we can obtain the SBS $S_{t \in \mathbb{R ^{K \times d}$ through a fast search method.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: In our paper, we adopt a Simhash-based search method for its simplicity and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-113: The SimHash algorithm takes an item's embedding vector as input and transforms it through a hash function into a binary signature as output.
elements.sent-2411-114: Simhash adheres to the locality-sensitive property: if the input vectors are similar, then the output binary signatures will also be similar.
elements.sent-2411-115: Specifically, we employ a fixed random matrix $H \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times m}$ where the elements are generated randomly around 0.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Each row of $H$ is served as a random hash function.
elements.sent-2411-117: Through matrix multiplication and the sign function, item embedding $e_i$ and query embedding $q_{t}$ can be transformed into m-dimensional binary signatures $h_i, h_q \in \mathbb{R ^{1 \times m}$. \begin{equation}\label{E4} h_i = SimHash(e_i) = ReLU(Sign(e_i H)),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{E5} h_{q_t} = SimHash(q_{t}) = ReLU(Sign(q_{t} H)),\end{equation} Due to the locality-sensitive property, the similarity between an item and a query can be replaced by the similarity between their binary signatures.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Therefore, we can use the Hamming distance between the query and item binary signatures as a similarity indicator instead of the inner product of the embedding vectors, thereby reducing the time complexity of calculating similarity.
elements.sent-2411-119: By comparing the Hamming distances of all behaviors to the query, we obtain the indices $I_t \in \mathbb{N ^{K}$ of the $K$ behaviors with the smallest Hamming distances to the query signature. \begin{equation}\label{E6} I_t = TopK (HammingDistance(h_i, h_{q_t})),\end{equation} The sub-sequence $S_{t}$ is obtained through $I_t$ when employing Simhash as the search algorithm: \begin{equation}\label{E7} S_{t} = Search(S, q_{t};SimHash) = Gather(S, I_t).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Let's compare the time complexity of computing the Hamming distance and the inner product of vectors.
elements.sent-2411-121: Assuming that multiplication is considered an atomic operation, the complexity of computing the inner product of two d-dimensional vectors is $O(d)$.
elements.sent-2411-122: In contrast, computing the Hamming distance between two m-dimensional signatures is approximated as $O(1)$ because this calculation can leverage the XOR operation.
elements.sent-2411-123: Therefore, our method significantly reduces the time complexity from $O(d)$ to $O(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Additionally, when model training updates item embeddings, the locality-sensitive property of SimHash ensures that the new binary signatures align with the updated item embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-125: Therefore, our search method improves the computational efficiency and ensures accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-126: In this unit, we broaden our focus beyond a single item.
elements.sent-2411-127: We make the granularity of observation coarser by using the user's recent behavior sequence to construct the query.
elements.sent-2411-128: Based on this query, which contains recent behavior information, we obtain a subsequence that represents the user's short-term interest, referred to as local-aware interest.
elements.sent-2411-129: It is worth noting that we do not directly use the recent behavior sequence as the SBS, as it may contain noise such as accidental clicks.
elements.sent-2411-130: Additionally, the user's long-term behavior sequence may include other behaviors that align with the local-aware interest.
elements.sent-2411-131: Specifically, we represent the user's recent behavior sequence as $S_r = [e_{L-J , \dots, e_L]$, where $J$ is the length of recent behavior sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Using average pooling to construct queries is a good choice.
elements.sent-2411-133: However, considering that $J$ is very small, we can further model $S_r$ to enhance the representation ability of the query with slightly increasing the computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-134: Considering that each behavior is a carrier of current potential interest, we use a GRU to extract a series of hidden states $H_r \in \mathbb{R ^{J \times d}$ from $S_r$.}
elements.sent-2411-135: We then combine $H_r$ and $S_r$ to get the query $q_l$.
elements.sent-2411-136: Since $q_l$ mines the semantic information of the user's recent behaviors, $S_l$ retrieved through $q_l$ align with the user's local-aware interest, which is a coarser-grained interest.
elements.sent-2411-137: In this unit, we expand the focus to encompass the entire behavior sequence to consider the user's more global and stable interest.
elements.sent-2411-138: Constructing a query using the user's entire long-term behavior sequence is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-139: On the one hand, directly average pooling the long sequence may result in poor query representation due to its length and the noise within the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-140: On the other hand, constructing the query with more complex modeling of the long sequence cannot meet the strict time limits of an online system.
elements.sent-2411-141: Additionally, for new users with relatively short behavior sequences, it is difficult to obtain their stable global and stable preferences.
elements.sent-2411-142: To address the above issues, we employ clustering methods to aggregate the item set $M$ into several clusters using pre-trained item embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-143: In this search unit, we construct a query by mapping the target item to the nearest cluster centroid.
elements.sent-2411-144: This query can efficiently and roughly capture the user's global preferences.
elements.sent-2411-145: Additionally, leveraging the semantic information of pre-trained embeddings helps to partially solve the user cold start problem.
elements.sent-2411-146: We adopt a simplified Density Peak Clustering~\cite{dpc (DPC) algorithm that doesn't require multiple iterations or additional parameters, efficiently identifying clusters of arbitrary shapes based on density connectivity.}
elements.sent-2411-147: DPC assumes that a cluster center is surrounded by low-density neighbors and maintains a relatively large distance from high-density points.
elements.sent-2411-148: For each item, we calculate its density $\rho$ and the minimum distance $\delta$ to items with higher density. \begin{equation}\rho_i = exp(-\sum_{e_j \in M}||e_i - e_j||^2_2),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\delta_i = \begin{cases} \text{min}_{j:\rho_j > \rho_i} \ ||e_i - e_j||_2 , \text{if} \ \exists j \ \text{s.t.}\ \rho_j > \rho_i \text{max}_j \ ||e_i - e_j||_2 , \text{otherwise} \end{cases},\end{equation} The cluster center score of an item is set to $\rho \times \delta$.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\delta_i =  min_{j:\rho_j > \rho_i} \ ||e_i - e_j||_2 , if \ \exists j \ s.t.\ \rho_j > \rho_i max_j \ ||e_i - e_j||_2 , otherwise ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-149: DPC selects $C$ items with the highest scores as cluster centers and assigns the remaining items to the closest cluster center.
elements.sent-2411-150: In this unit, we construct the query $q_{g}$ using the aforementioned clustering algorithm and average pooling.
elements.sent-2411-151: The query $q_{g}$ guides the retrieval of the relevant SBS $S_{g \in \mathbb{R ^{K \times d}$ using the same SimHash method as described in \crefrange{E4 {E7 : \begin{equation}q_{g} = DPC(e_{t}) + Avg(S),\end{equation} \begin{equation}S_{g} = Search(S, q_{g}; SimHash).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Since the cluster assignment for each item is precomputed, the complexity of it is $O(1)$ during inference.
elements.sent-2411-153: Since $q_g$ contains the semantic information of the user's global preferences, $S_g$ retrieved through $q_g$ align with the user's global-aware interest, which is the most coarse-grained interest.
elements.sent-2411-154: After obtaining the subsequences that contains the user's multi-granularity interests, we aim to further mine sequential and interactive information to more accurately learn the user's diverse interests.
elements.sent-2411-155: For convenience, we unify $S_t,S_l,S_g$ as $S_* \in \mathbb{R ^{K \times d}$.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Previous work has rarely emphasized the order of behaviors in SBSs, possibly sorting them by relevance in the retrieval module.
elements.sent-2411-157: However, we believe that the sequential information within SBSs plays a crucial role, much like the original sequence, in helping us better understand and learn the user's interests.
elements.sent-2411-158: Therefore, we preserve the original sequence of behaviors in SBS and use position encoding to supplement the sequential relationship, compensate for the information loss of the retrieval module, and enhance the expressive power of $S_*$.
elements.sent-2411-159: We use the relative position of each behavior in $S_*$ to the target item to construct a position encoding, termed target-aware position encoding.
elements.sent-2411-160: Rather than the widely used chronological order encoding~\cite{SAS , this method better emphasizes the attention on the target information.}
elements.sent-2411-161: Specifically, we propose the Target-Aware Position Encoding $TPE \in \mathbb{R ^{L \times d}$.}
elements.sent-2411-162: We then convert the retrieved indices $I$ in equation~\eqref{E6 to target-aware indices $I'$.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Finally, we obtain the target-aware position embeddings through a look-up process and incorporate them into $S_*$: \begin{equation}S_* = S_* + lookup(TPE, I')).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-164: After applying the above TAPE, our model has learned the sequential information in SBSs.
elements.sent-2411-165: To efficiently learn the interactive information with less additional computational cost, we leverage the efficient Fourier Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-166: According to the convolution theorem~\cite{C , the product operator in the frequency domain is equivalent to global circular convolution in the time domain.}
elements.sent-2411-167: This implies that complex convolution operations can be implemented through fast Fourier transform (FFT) with a time complexity of $O(KlogK)$ and multiplication operations~\cite{fnet, gnet .}
elements.sent-2411-168: Compared with the self-attention mechanism with a time complexity of $O(K^2)$, this method explores interactive information while improving efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-169: Specifically, we perform Fourier transform along the sequence length dimension to transform $S_*$ to $X \in \mathbb{C ^{K \times d}$ which represents the frequency domain representation of $S_*$.}
elements.sent-2411-170: We then implement the convolution fusion of behaviors and mine interactive information by performing a dot product with the complex parameter matrix $W \in \mathbb{C ^{K \times d}$~\cite{fmlp .}}
elements.sent-2411-171: Finally, we obtain the full convolution sequence representation $\widetilde{S_* \in \mathbb{R ^{K \times d}$ through inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT).}}
elements.sent-2411-172: The formulas are as follows: \begin{equation}\label{E16} X = \mathcal{F} (S_*), \quad \widetilde{X} = W \odot X, \quad \widetilde{S_*} = \mathcal{F} (\widetilde{X}),\end{equation} Additionally, inspired by the multi-head self attention mechanism, we further propose the Multi-Head Fourier Transformer.

elements.sent-2411-174: Then we shares weights $W\in \mathbb{C ^{ d \times d}$ among different tokens, to adaptively handle sequences of different lengths while reducing the parameter count.}
elements.sent-2411-175: To mine the interactive information from different subspaces, we divide $X \in \mathbb{C ^{K \times d}$ into $n$ parts $x_i \in \mathbb{C ^{K \times d/n}$ and diagonalize the weight $W\in \mathbb{C ^{ d \times d}$ into $n$ blocks $W_i \in \mathbb{C ^{d / n \times d / n}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: Each block of $X$ and the diagonal matrix is equivalent to a head of the multi-head self attention.
elements.sent-2411-177: The diagonal weights can be computed in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-178: We implement them through an MLP with an activation function $\sigma$ to introduce nonlinear relationships.

elements.sent-2411-273: In this paper, we proposed the Multi- granularity Interest Retrieval and Refinement Network (MIRRN) for long-term user behavior modeling in CTR prediction.
elements.sent-2411-274: To the best of our knowledge, MIRRN is the first to successfully capture the user’s multi-granularity interests.
elements.sent-2411-275: Specifically, we empolyed Multi-granularity Interest Retrieval Module to extract subsequences containing interests of different granularities.
elements.sent-2411-276: We then utilized Target-Aware Position Encoding and Multi-Head Fourier Transformer to efficiently and effectively mine sequential information and interactive information within these subsequences.
elements.sent-2411-277: Finally, we used multi-head target attention to adaptively learn the influence of different granular interests on the target item.
elements.sent-2411-278: Experiments on three public datasets, along with an online A/B test validated the effectiveness of MIRRN.
elements.sent-2411-279: In the future, we plan to consider user interests at additional granularities tailored to different scenarios and explore more efficient retrieval methods to further reduce time complexity.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}

elements.sent-2411-410: In this paper, we proposed the Multi-granularity Interest Retrieval and Refinement Network (MIRRN) for long-term user behavior modeling in CTR prediction.
elements.sent-2411-411: To the best of our knowledge, MIRRN is the first to successfully capture, refine, and integrate the user’s multi-granularity interests.
elements.sent-2411-412: Specifically, we constructed multi-granularity queries, extracting subsequences that capture diverse interests.
elements.sent-2411-413: We then utilized Target-Aware Position Encoding and Multi-Head Fourier Transformer to efficiently and effectively mine sequential information and interactive information within these subsequences, obtaining the refined interest vectors.
elements.sent-2411-414: Finally, we employed multi-head target attention to adaptively learn the influence of different granular interests on the target item.
elements.sent-2411-415: In the future, we plan to consider user interests at additional granularities tailored to different scenarios and explore more efficient retrieval methods to further reduce time complexity.
